comments.text: Brayden and Sawhney's paper is now on arxiv!

Following Mehtaab Sawhney remarks, I was able with GPT 5.2 (with a small input from Gemini/Grok) to complete the argument. Basically giving various estimates with $\limsup$ being straightforward and $\liminf$ being more tricky. You can find the full write-up of the arguments here. $\limsup$ and $\liminf$ parts are indepentent. Last section is the summary of results (also below), and 6.6. considers M.S. restatement in the comments below. For Lebesgue-a.e. $t\in(0,1)$ the signs $a_k(t)=(-1)^{\epsilon_k(t)}$ are i.i.d. Rademacher, and\[ M_n(t)=\max_{x\in[-1,1]}\Big|\sum_{k=1}^n a_k(t)x^k\Big| \]admits a sharp almost-sure upper-envelope law:\[ \limsup_{n\to\infty}\frac{M_n(t)}{\sqrt{2n\log\log n}}=1. \]The proof is a reduction by Abel summation (Salem-Zygmund style) to maxima of two simple random walks, followed by the classical LIL (Kolmogorov-Hartman-Wintner/Chung). The same comparison yields subgaussian tails at the $\sqrt n$ scale for each fixed $n$. For the lower envelope, the random-walk reduction is one-sided and gives only\[ \liminf_{n\to\infty}\Big(\tfrac{\log\log n}{n}\Big)^{1/2}M_n(t)\le \frac{\pi}{\sqrt8}, \]with no matching reverse bound. A more sensitive viewpoint uses the endpoint parametrization $x=\pm e^{-u/n}$, under which $M_n/\sqrt n$ becomes the supremum of two Laplace-type processes $Z_n^\pm(u)$. Using a two-dimensional strong invariance principle (KMT/Zaitsev-type), $(Z_n^+,Z_n^-)$ can be coupled to two independent copies of $Y(u)=\int_0^1 e^{-us}\,dB(s)$; the small-deviation asymptotics of Gao-Li-Wellner, $\log\mathbb P(\sup_{u\ge0}|Y(u)|\le\epsilon)\asymp-|\log\epsilon|^3$, then give a Borel-Cantelli dichotomy on sparse subsequences showing that the minimal values occur on the scale\[ M_n(t)=\sqrt n\,\exp\!\big(-\Theta((\log\log n)^{1/3})\big)\quad\text{infinitely often}, \]with threshold constants determined (up to two-sided bounds) by the small-ball constants for $Y$.

Thanks - LLM-generated proofs like this are prone to containing subtle mistakes, especially when invoking tools from the literature, so I will view this as still unresolved until either an expert can take a look and confirm this, or a formalised proof is available.

Of course! Looking for an expert opinion as well. Nevertheless there are basically 3 independent parts: limsup, liminf and tails. On the other hand, I'm running it through Aristotle AI for a potential formalization but I doubt these kind of analytics results are currently likely to be formalized.

(1) This proof is correct (and is what I suggested in the sketch below). The lim sup argument is actually immediate (?) given the LLL so the lower bound is the only tricky part. The sketch suggested is implemented correctly with KMT. (2) Actually since I posted (and hence I marked working on the problem) Brayden Letwin, an undergraduate at University of Alberta, reached out and pointed out this KMT argument to me and has worked out this sketch several weeks ago. We together can actually prove a much stronger result (e.g. we can improve the key Gao-Li-Wellner to determine the constant in front of of $\log(1/\varepsilon)^3$ which eventually translates to an constant in front of the $\Theta$ obtained in the above result). I will attempt to finish the writeup as soon as possible. (3) While the result mentioned in (2) is arguably quite a satisfactory resolution, one could argue (especially reading the paper of Salem--Zygmund) that one should aim for an LLL type result which determines this minimum to within a $1+o(1)$. While we won't publish this argument, it turns out if one can obtain a sufficiently strong error term in the Gao-Li-Wellner this would be possible. However this looks like a formidable problem in the theory of Gaussian processes.
Thank you for the reply. In other words, the problem is solved, either by my note above (flashing out your line of thought) or by your stronger result that is coming, right?

Neither of these results pins down he answer as precisely as Salem--Zygmund might have wanted. (Our result will determine the optimal function to $\exp(o(\log\log n)^{2/3})$ but one may ask for an extremely tight control which is likely true.

It took me a while to understand what exactly this problem is attempting to ask. Note that for a fixed scale $n$ that the maximum will be around size $\sqrt{n}$. To see the lower bound, one can just examine the endpoint $x = 1$. For the upper bound one can use an argument of Salem--Zygmund that\[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\varepsilon_k x^{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(x^{k} - x^{k+1})\Big(\sum_{j=1}^{k}\varepsilon_j\Big) + x^n\Big(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\varepsilon_j\Big).\]Now for $x\in [0,1]$, these coefficients are nonnegative and together sum to $1$ so\[\max_{x\in [0,1]}\Big|\sum_{k=1}^{n}\varepsilon_k x^{k}\Big|\le \max_{k\le n}\Big|\sum_{j=1}^{k}\varepsilon_j\Big|.\]Via standard bounds for the maximum of a random walk, we have that the probability this is $\ge \lambda n^{1/2}$ scales as $e^{-\Omega(\lambda^2)}$. An essentially identical analysis applies for $x\in [-1,0]$ modulo replacing $\varepsilon_j$ by $(-1)^{j}\varepsilon_j$. My current guess for what Erdos is asking for is a function $f(n)$ such that for almost all $t$ we have that $M_n(t)\ge f(n)$ for all but finitely many $n$ but $M_n(t)\ge C f(n)$ for infinitely many $n$. For this problem, note that\[\mathbb{P}\Big[\max_{k\le n}\Big|\sum_{j=1}^{k}\varepsilon_j\Big|\le \varepsilon n^{1/2}\Big]\asymp \exp(-\Omega(1/\epsilon^2)).\]Noting that for scales which are sufficiently far away (e.g. $n$ vs $n(\log n)^2$ say) these two events are essentially independent. Now as there are $(\log n)^{1-o(1)}$ such distinct scales up to $n$, setting $\varepsilon \approx 1/\sqrt{\log\log n}$ we essentially recover Chung's result.

This problem it turns out is very likely to follow from arguments present in the literature already; the true scale of this minimum seems to be between $n^{1/2}\exp(-c(\log\log n)^{1/3})$ and $n^{1/2}\exp(-c'(\log\log n)^{1/3})$. (Determining the exact constant $c$ is likely more technical.) The crucial point is to reparameterize $x = e^{-t/n}$ (thinking of $t$ fixed as $n\to \infty$) and we are seeing to bound\[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\varepsilon_k e^{-tk/n}\]for $t\ge 0$. This is essentially equivalent to looking at $Y(t) =\Big| \int_{0}^{1}e^{-tx}dB(x)\Big|$ and looking at the maximum for $t\ge 0$. We want to understand the probability that this maximum is small; this scaling is worked out in https://arxiv.org/pdf/1001.0200. (This reference was located with help from ChatGPT 5.2 Pro.) The remaining technical work is passing between the Gaussian limit and this problem.

I just read [SZ54, p. 299] carefully, and I believe the current formulation of this problem on the website is incorrect; as stated (with coefficients $\epsilon_k(t)\in\{0,1\}$ and $x\in[0,1]$) it becomes essentially trivial. (indeed, the polynomial has nonnegative coefficients, so it is increasing on $[0,1]$, and thus \(M_n(t)=\max_{0\le x\le 1}\sum_{k\le n}\epsilon_k(t)x^k=\sum_{k\le n}\epsilon_k(t)\), reducing the question to the law of large numbers for the dyadic digits). According to [SZ54], the intended coefficients should be random signs, i.e. Rademacher functions $\varphi_k(t)\in\{\pm1\}$ rather than binary digits $\epsilon_k(t)\in\{0,1\}$. In terms of the dyadic expansion $t=\sum_{k\ge1}\epsilon_k(t)2^{-k}$, one can set for instance\[ \varphi_k(t)=1-2\epsilon_k(t)\in\{\pm1\} \qquad\text{or}\qquad \varphi_k(t)=(-1)^{\epsilon_k(t)}. \]Moreover, the maximization should be over $x\in[-1,1]$, not just $x\in[0,1]$. In other words, the Salem--Zygmund version is\[ M_n(t)=\max_{x\in[-1,1]}\left|\sum_{k\le n}\varphi_k(t)\,x^k\right|. \]

Thanks, I agree (the $[0,1]$ in place of $[-1,1]$ is my typo not Erdős' - the conflation of the $\{0,1\}$ vs $\{-1,1\}$ model is an unfortunate ambiguity that has come up in other problems from [Er61]).
